steps.action: The farandole (French: [faʁɑ̃dɔl]; Provençal: farandola [faʀanˈdulɔ]) is an open-chain community dance popular in Provence, France.
steps.sourceSnippet: The farandole (French: [faʁɑ̃dɔl]; Provençal: farandola [faʀanˈdulɔ]) is an open-chain community dance popular in Provence, France.

steps.action: A still more unlikely derivation has been suggested from the Greek fálanx (φάλαγξ, "phalanx") and doúlos (δούλος, "slave"), because the dancers in the farandole are linked together in a long chain.
steps.sourceSnippet: A still more unlikely derivation has been suggested from the Greek fálanx (φάλαγξ, "phalanx") and doúlos (δούλος, "slave"), because the dancers in the farandole are linked together in a long chain.

steps.action: The dance is very probably of Greek origin, and seems to be a direct descendant of the "Cranes' dance", the invention of which was acribed to Theseus, who instituted it to celebrate his escape from the Labyrinth.
steps.sourceSnippet: The dance is very probably of Greek origin, and seems to be a direct descendant of the "Cranes' dance", the invention of which was acribed to Theseus, who instituted it to celebrate his escape from the Labyrinth.

steps.action: This dance is alluded to at the end of the hymn to Delos by Callimachus: it is still danced in Greece and the islands of the Aegean, and may well have been introduced into the South of France from Marseilles.
steps.sourceSnippet: This dance is alluded to at the end of the hymn to Delos by Callimachus: it is still danced in Greece and the islands of the Aegean, and may well have been introduced into the South of France from Marseilles.

steps.action: Many recent websites, older encyclopedias, and some music history books claim that the farandole is a medieval dance, but never provide an actual medieval quote mentioning the farandole.
steps.sourceSnippet: Many recent websites, older encyclopedias, and some music history books claim that the farandole is a medieval dance, but never provide an actual medieval quote mentioning the farandole.

steps.action: Consequently, the medieval dance researcher Robert Mullally concludes that there is no evidence that the modern folk farandole resembles any kind of medieval dance.
steps.sourceSnippet: Consequently, the medieval dance researcher Robert Mullally concludes that there is no evidence that the modern folk farandole resembles any kind of medieval dance.

steps.action: In the village of Belvédère, on the occasion of the festival honouring patron Saint Blaise, the most recently married couple leads the dance.
steps.sourceSnippet: In the village of Belvédère, on the occasion of the festival honouring patron Saint Blaise, the most recently married couple leads the dance.

steps.action: "Musically, the dance is in 68 time, with a strongly accentuated rhythm, moderate to fast tempo, and played by a flute and drum.
steps.sourceSnippet: "Musically, the dance is in 68 time, with a strongly accentuated rhythm, moderate to fast tempo, and played by a flute and drum.

steps.action: With his left hand the leader holds the hand of his partner, in his right he waves a flag, handkerchief, or ribbon, which serves as a signal for his followers.
steps.sourceSnippet: With his left hand the leader holds the hand of his partner, in his right he waves a flag, handkerchief, or ribbon, which serves as a signal for his followers.

steps.action: As the Faraudole proceeds through the streets of the town the string of dancers is constantly recruited by fresh additions.
steps.sourceSnippet: As the Faraudole proceeds through the streets of the town the string of dancers is constantly recruited by fresh additions.

steps.action: The leader (to quote the poet Mistral) 'makes it come and go, turn backwards and forwards .
steps.sourceSnippet: The leader (to quote the poet Mistral) 'makes it come and go, turn backwards and forwards .

steps.action: sometimes he forms it into a ring, sometimes winds it in a spiral, then he breaks off from his followers and dances in front, then he joins on again, and makes it pass rapidly under the uplifted arms of the last couple.
steps.sourceSnippet: sometimes he forms it into a ring, sometimes winds it in a spiral, then he breaks off from his followers and dances in front, then he joins on again, and makes it pass rapidly under the uplifted arms of the last couple.

steps.action: In these rituals, music and dance played the leading roles.
steps.sourceSnippet: In these rituals, music and dance played the leading roles.

steps.action: The number of characters and the composition of the dance depends on the place of creation.
steps.sourceSnippet: The number of characters and the composition of the dance depends on the place of creation.

steps.action: Death, most often represented with a musical instrument, draws everyone into the dance, not looking at rank, wealth, gender, or age.
steps.sourceSnippet: Death, most often represented with a musical instrument, draws everyone into the dance, not looking at rank, wealth, gender, or age.

steps.action: The dancers join hands to form a wandering open chain and mark each beat with jumps.
steps.sourceSnippet: The dancers join hands to form a wandering open chain and mark each beat with jumps.

steps.action: The leader guides the chain by drawing a snake shape.
steps.sourceSnippet: The leader guides the chain by drawing a snake shape.

steps.action: Its role is to articulate the farandole, dance of agrarian rites, in its two main themes: that of the spiral (also known as the snail or labyrinth) and that of the passage under the vault (known as the serpent).
steps.sourceSnippet: Its role is to articulate the farandole, dance of agrarian rites, in its two main themes: that of the spiral (also known as the snail or labyrinth) and that of the passage under the vault (known as the serpent).

steps.action: Also in Provence, other dances related to the farandole were practiced on more free steps: the brandi, the morisca ("Moorish"), the passa-carriera ("street passer", cf.
steps.sourceSnippet: Also in Provence, other dances related to the farandole were practiced on more free steps: the brandi, the morisca ("Moorish"), the passa-carriera ("street passer", cf.

steps.action: It gave birth to certain medieval dances with repeated steps, such as the caroles of the 13th and 14th centuries, the branles of the 15th and 16th centuries.
steps.sourceSnippet: It gave birth to certain medieval dances with repeated steps, such as the caroles of the 13th and 14th centuries, the branles of the 15th and 16th centuries.

steps.action: The dancers form a long line which moves by meandering.
steps.sourceSnippet: The dancers form a long line which moves by meandering.

steps.action: The twists and turns of this dance in the crèche must represent a labyrinth.
steps.sourceSnippet: The twists and turns of this dance in the crèche must represent a labyrinth.

steps.action: The dancers are either dressed in traditional Arles, Provençal, or Comtadin clothing, with the different clothing nuances provided by the local santonnier.
steps.sourceSnippet: The dancers are either dressed in traditional Arles, Provençal, or Comtadin clothing, with the different clothing nuances provided by the local santonnier.

steps.action: However, the dance is not suited for the purposes of the ballet.
steps.sourceSnippet: However, the dance is not suited for the purposes of the ballet.
